heading: TONUS SEXTUS — De modo procedendi, et concordia generali hominis cum mundo maiori, et Archetypo. Caput I
card_summary: This opening chapter of the Sixth Tone establishes Zorzi's methodological prologue for the section on the concordance of man with the macrocosm and the Archetype. Invoking Apollonius of Tyana (identified as a Pythagorean) on the nature of painting as imitation of nature, Zorzi argues that all describers of reality — poets, orators, historians, and theologians — should strive for faithful imitation over artificial verbal ornament. Moses is held up as the supreme exemplar of this principle: inspired by God, he described the construction of both worlds (macrocosm and microcosm/man) with a mode of writing fitted to each subject's dignity. The macrocosm was produced by divine fiat alone, while man — the lesser world that is nonetheless greater in virtue and mystery — was fashioned with incomparably greater care, as shown by God's prior preparation of his dwelling, his dominion over living creatures, his garden of delights, his companion, and his inheritance of all things.
essay:
## Overview and Methodological Framing

The opening chapter of the *Tonus Sextus* announces its subject as *"concordia generali hominis cum mundo maiori, et Archetypo"* — the general concordance of man with the greater world and with the Archetype. Before engaging that theme directly, Zorzi pauses to reflect on the *modus procedendi* — the proper mode of proceeding — a methodological prologue that is characteristic of his self-conscious literary practice.

## The Analogy of Painting: Apollonius the Pythagorean

Zorzi opens with an appeal to the visual arts and to a Pythagorean authority:

> Pictores naturae aemuli accuratissimi ea, quae figurant, quoad possunt, sedulo imitantur. Nam pictura (inquit Apollonius pythagoreus) est imitatio quaedam rei naturalis, a natura comparata, sed colorum compositio est ab arte. In prima autem potior pingendi vis collocata est.

<cite>p. 750</cite>

The citation is attributed to *Apollonius pythagoreus* — Apollonius of Tyana, the Neopythagorean philosopher and wonder-worker whose *Vita* by Philostratus was well known in Renaissance humanist circles. Zorzi uses the Apollonian dictum to establish a hierarchy: the imitative force (*potior pingendi vis*) grounded in nature is more powerful than the compositional artifice of color arrangement, which is mere technique. This mirrors a standard Renaissance ut pictura poesis topos, but Zorzi immediately redirects it toward a theological epistemology.

## Imitatio as the Duty of All Describers

The principle is generalized from painters to all who describe reality — *veri descriptores*, whether poets, orators, or historiographers:

> Et quod faciunt illi coloribus, itidem pro virili sua et veri descriptores facere conantur, sive poetae, sive oratores, sive historiographi sint, in quibus omnibus magis imitatio, et plena rerum descriptio, quam natura tribuit, quam artificiosa characterum compositio commendatur.

<cite>p. 750</cite>

The evaluative criterion is clear: *imitatio* and *plena rerum descriptio* — faithful, full description of things as nature gives them — is to be preferred over *artificiosa characterum compositio*, ornamental verbal construction. This is Zorzi positioning himself as a practitioner of theological natural description rather than scholastic disputation or humanist rhetoric for its own sake.

## Moses as the Supreme Exemplar

The argument converges on Moses, *Deo plenus*, as the paradigm case of a writer whose mode of writing was fitted to the dignity of his subject:

> Ideo, cum nos descriptores simus operum summi Artificis, aequum est, ut iis, quae exaramus, scribendi modus pro viribus congruat, cuius rei noster Moses Deo plenus extat exemplar clarissimum, qui utriusque mundi fabricam scribens, pro ut illorum uterque exquirebat, accommodata cura contexuit.

<cite>p. 750</cite>

The phrase *noster Moses* is theologically loaded: Moses is "ours" in the sense that he belongs to the Christian Kabbalist tradition Zorzi is heir to. The claim is that Moses, in writing the account of creation (*utriusque mundi fabricam*), adapted his narrative care to the nature of each world he was describing — macrocosm and microcosm alike.

## The Two Worlds and Their Respective Dignity

Zorzi then draws the central contrast that will animate the *Tonus Sextus*. The macrocosm was brought forth by the bare divine command (*solo iussu productas*), as Genesis narrates ("Let there be..."). But when Moses turns to man, the lesser world (*minor mundus*) who is paradoxically greater in virtue and mystery (*maior est virtute et mysterio*), the narrative mode shifts:

> Ad minorem vero mundum deveniens (qui tamen maior est virtute et mysterio) eius opificium tanto celebriori artificio describit, quanto ipse est excellentiori munere fabricatus.

<cite>p. 750–751</cite>

The evidence of this heightened care is enumerated through a series of divine preparations: God prepared a dwelling (*domus*) before man arrived; he granted man dominion (*ditio*) over all living creatures; he planted a garden of delight (*amoenitatis locus omnibus delitiis consitus*) for man's pleasure; he gave man a most pleasant companion (*socia iucundissima*); and he donated all things to man's governance as to a lawful heir (*legitimo haeredi*).

## Cosmological and Kabbalistic Stakes

This passage serves as the programmatic entry-point for the *Tonus Sextus* as a whole, which will elaborate the *concordia* between man (the microcosm), the macrocosm, and the Archetype. The triadic structure — Archetype / macrocosm / microcosm — is Zorzi's persistent Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic scaffold throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*. The present chapter establishes that man's position in this schema is not merely the lowest of the three but in a meaningful sense the highest in terms of *virtus* and *mysterium*, because man participates most directly in the Archetypal order.

The appeal to Apollonius of Tyana alongside Moses is notable: Zorzi routinely frames his project as a convergence of Pythagorean, Platonic, and Mosaic wisdom — the *prisca theologia* model inherited from Ficino and Pico. By opening the sixth tone with this double citation, he signals that the cosmological anthropology to follow will be grounded in both the philosophical tradition and Scripture read through an elevated, spiritually perceptive hermeneutic.
